# Pd/IPr^BIDEA 催化 gem-二氟环丙烷区域选择性氢化脱氟合成末端氟代烯烃

## 引用信息

- **标题**：Pd/IPr^BIDEA-Catalyzed Hydrodefluorination of gem-Difluorocyclopropanes: Regioselective Synthesis of Terminal Fluoroalkenes
- **作者**：Huijun Qian, Zachary P. Cheng, Yani Luo, Leiyang Lv*, Shuming Chen*, and **Zhiping Li**（李志平，通讯作者）*

## 正文

# Pd/IPr<sup>BIDEA</sup>-Catalyzed Hydrodefluorination of gem-Difluorocyclopropanes: Regioselective Synthesis of Terminal Fluoroalkenes

Huijun Qian,<sup>†</sup> Zachary P. Cheng,<sup>†</sup> Yani Luo, Leiyang Lv,\* Shuming Chen,\* and Zhiping Li\*

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

Cite This: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2024, 146, 24−32

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

Read Online

ACCESS

Metrics & More

Article Recommendations

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

\*sı Supporting Information

ABSTRACT: Developing new strategies to enable chemo- and regioselective reductions is an important topic in chemical research. Herein, an eficient and regioselective $\mathrm { P d / I P r ^ { B I D E A } }$ -catalyzed ring-opening hydrodefluorination of gem-difluorocyclopropanes to access terminal fluoroalkenes is developed. The success of this transformation was attributed to the use of 3,3-dimethylallyl Bpin as a novel hydride donor. DFT calculations suggest that a direct 3,4′-hydride transfer via a 9-membered cyclic transition state is more favorable, which combined with the irreversibility of the reaction enables the unusual selectivity for the less thermodynamically stable terminal alkene isomer. This reaction mode is also applicable to a variety of regioselective allylic and propargyl reductions.

P <sup>alladium-catalyzed</sup> <sup>allylic</sup> <sup>alkylation,</sup> <sup>also</sup> <sup>known</sup> <sup>as</sup> <sup>the</sup> Tsuji−Trost reaction, has been widely used in synthetic organic chemistry and catalysis for decades.1 This reaction mode is characterized by the formation of a π-allyl palladium complex followed by nucleophilic attack, and many powerfu transformations involving diferent types of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen nucleophiles have been developed accordingly.<sup>2</sup> When a hydride is used as the nucleophile, the allylic reduction afords alkenes, which are particularly useful when the reduction is regioselective.<sup>3</sup> Typically, mixtures of internal and terminal alkenes are obtained with common hydride donors, and internal alkenes are the major products in most cases due to their higher thermodynamic stability (calcd 5−6 kcal/mol) (Scheme 1A). In addition, over-reduction to alkanes needs to be avoided, and functional group compatibility also has to be considered. Regioselective synthesis of thermody namically less stable terminal alkenes via allylic reduction has therefore remained a challenging task to date.<sup>4</sup>

Allyl boronates are useful synthetic reagents in a variety of organic transformations, including the Suzuki coupling, the Petasis reaction, the Chan−Lam coupling, and the allylboration of carbonyls, imines, $\mathrm { C O } _ { 2 }$ (π-electrophiles), etc. in which the organoboronates serve as carbon nucleophiles.<sup>5</sup> In addition, allyl boronates also act as radical acceptors in $^ { 1 , 3 - }$ difunctionalization reactions via intramolecular 1,2-boron shift.<sup>6</sup> Despite these elegant and powerful approaches, the use of allyl boronic esters as hydride donors has not been previously reported (Scheme 1B).

Recently, gem-difluorocyclopropanes $\left( g e m  – \mathrm { F } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C P s } \right)$ , which bear two fluorine atoms on the same carbon of the parent cyclopropane ring, have attracted great interest in the biopharmaceutical sciences and led to the development of a variety of challenging and creative transformations.<sup>7</sup> One of the most important synthetic applications of gem-F CPs is transition-metal-catalyzed ring-opening to deliver various 2- fluorinated allyl derivatives, as demonstrated by Fu and co workers in the Pd(0)-catalyzed regioselective activation of F CPs followed by trapping with $\mathrm { N } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { \Gamma } } } } , \mathrm { O } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { \Gamma } } } } ,$ and C-nucleophiles. Subsequently, transition-metal-catalyzed ring-opening func tionalization of gem-F CPs with various nucleophiles or electrophiles has become well-established.<sup>9</sup> In particular, hydrodefluorination (HDF)<sup>10</sup> of gem-F CPs by hydride transfer reagents could provide convenient access to monofluoroalkenes, which are useful as enol or amide mimics in medicinal chemistry.<sup>11</sup> However, this synthetic route has been much less explored than with other nucleophiles. As early as 1983, Isogai and co-workers reported the first catalytic hydrogenolysis of gem-F CPs over a heterogeneous catalyst such as PdO or Raney nickel, albeit lacking chemoselectivity. 12 In 2016, Gade and co-workers disclosed an elegant ring opening and hydrodefluorination reaction of gem-F CPs catalyzed by NNN-pincer-Ni (II) fluorido complexes using Me NHBH as the hydride donor (Scheme 1C).<sup>13</sup> This transformation aforded internal fluoroalkenes in good yields and with moderate Z-selectivity, and a radical-based mecha nism was proposed that involved the interplay between Ni(I) complexes and Ni(II) hydrido species. However, a selective synthesis of terminal fluoroalkenes has remained elusive, which is partly attributable to the lower stability of terminal alkenes compared to internal ones due to the hyperconjugation efect. Terminal alkenes incorporating fluorine atoms, in particular, are therefore sought-after synthetic targets.14

Our lab has been focused on the development of regio- and stereoselective ring-opening functionalizations of gem-F CPs using well-defined palladium catalysts.<sup>15</sup> Using gem-difluor-

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

## Scheme 1. Catalytic Allylic Reduction and Ring-Opening Hydrodefluorination of gem-Difluorocyclopropanes

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

ocyclopropanes (gem-F CPs) as the model substrates, we herein report a proof-of-concept use of 1,1-dimethylallyl boronate as a hydride donor in the regioselective allylic reductions (Scheme 1D). DFT calculations and mechanistic data indicate that a new reaction mode−direct $^ { 3 , 4 ^ { \prime } }$ -hydride transfer−is responsible for the unusual regioselectivity.<sup>16</sup> This transformation provides a wide range of valuable terminal fluoroalkene structures. Furthermore, this unprecedented protocol has been applied to regioselective hydrodechlorination, hydrodeoxygenation, and hydrodesulfonation. A variety of allylic precursors, such as vinyl dioxolanone, vinyl oxazolidinone, vinyl oxirane, and propargylic ester, were successfully employed in the reaction.

Reaction Optimization. We started the reaction optimization with 1a as the model substrate and 3,3-dimethylallyl Bpin 2a as the hydride donor (Table 1). We first tested the XPhos-Pd-G3 catalyst, which has been widely used in the ringopening functionalization of gem-F CPs. However, terminal fluoroalkene 3a was aforded in only 18% yield along with 7% of 4a (entry 1). Based on our previous work, we then attempted the hydrodefluorination under the Pd/NHC catalytic system. We were pleased to find that the terminal alkene 3a could be obtained in 65% yield along with 4% of the internal fluoroalkene 4a in the presence of 2.5 mol % of $\mathbf { P d } { \cdot } 2 ,$ 1.5 equiv of 2a and with ${ \mathrm { C s } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C O } } _ { 3 }$ as the base under argon in THF at $1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 3 h (entry 2). We hypothesized that increasing the rate of the reductive elimination might lead to better selectivity for the kinetic product 3a, so we evaluated NHC ligands with diferent sizes of ortho substituents (Pd-3 to Pd-6). As expected, the yield of 3a was further improved (from 66% to 91%) with longer alkyl chains of the NHC arms (entries 3−6). However, a small amount of 4a was still obtained alongside 3a. We reasoned that steric modifications of the NHC skeleton that push the arms closer to the Pd 17 center could also help accelerate the reductive elimination. Catalyst Pd-7, with an acenaphthene-fused imidazole skeleton $( { \mathrm { P d } } { \mathrm { - I P r } } ^ { \mathrm { B I A N } }$ , BIAN = bis(imino)acenaphthene)<sup>18</sup> was synthesized and tested accordingly, which shows similar yield and selectivity to Pd-6 (entry 7). The Pd- $\mathbf { \cdot I P r ^ { B I D E A } }$ (BIDEA = bis(imino)-9,10-dihydro-9,10-ethenoanthracene, Pd-8) catalyst<sup>19</sup> with a bridged anthracene-fused imidazole skeleton gave the desired product 3a in 94% yield, with no 4a detected by crude <sup>1</sup>H NMR (entry 8). Changing the base to NaOH, LiO<sup>t</sup>Bu or K CO decreased the eficiency of the reaction (entries 9−11). Other solvents (MeCN, toluene, 1,4-dioxane and DMF) gave inferior results compared to THF (entries 12−15). Control experiments showed that the Pd catalyst and the base were necessary for this hydro-defluorination reaction (entries 16 and 17). In addition, 3a was obtained in 89% yield when the reaction was carried out on 1.0 mmol scale (entry 18).

Table 1. Optimization of Reaction Conditions ab  
![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

<table><tr><td>entry</td><td>catalyst</td><td>base</td><td>solvent</td><td>3a (%)</td><td>4a (%)</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Pd-1</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>18</td><td>7</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>Pd-2</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>65</td><td>4</td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>Pd-3</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>66</td><td>2</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Pd-4</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>68</td><td>2</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>Pd-5</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>85</td><td>3</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>Pd-6</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>91</td><td>3</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>Pd-7</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>92</td><td>2</td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>94</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>9</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $K_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>22</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td>Pd-8</td><td>NaOH</td><td>THF</td><td>72</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>11</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $LiO^{t}Bu$ </td><td>THF</td><td>42</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>12</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td> $CH_{3}CN$ </td><td></td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>13</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>toluene</td><td>47</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>14</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>1,4-dioxane</td><td>48</td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>15</td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>DMF</td><td></td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>16</td><td></td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td></td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td>17</td><td>Pd-8</td><td></td><td>THF</td><td></td><td>N.D.</td></tr><tr><td> $18^c$ </td><td>Pd-8</td><td> $Cs_{2}CO_{3}$ </td><td>THF</td><td>89</td><td>2</td></tr></table>

<sup>a</sup>Reaction conditions: 1a (0.1 mmol), 2a (0.15 mmol), Pd catalyst (2.5 mol %), base (0.2 mmol), solvent (1.0 mL), 100 $^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , 3$ h under Ar unless otherwise noted. <sup>b</sup>NMR yields were based on 1a and determined by <sup>1</sup>H NMR using mesitylene as an internal standard. The 3a/4a yields were determined by <sup>1</sup>H NMR analysis of the crude mixtures. <sup>c</sup>1.0 mmol scale. N.D. = not detected by <sup>1</sup>H and <sup>19</sup>F NMR.

Hydride Donor Evaluation. We tested a variety of common reducing agents, such as silanes, formic acid, ammonium formate (NEt ·HCOOH), ammonia borane $\left( \mathrm { N E t } _ { 3 } { \cdot } \mathrm { B H } _ { 3 } \right)$ , pinacolborane (HBpin), 9-borabicyclo(3.3.1)- nonane (9-BBN), isopropanol, Hantzsch ester, Hünig base (DIPEA), and NaH in the hydrodefluorination of gem-F CP (Table 2). However, lower yields or poor regioselectivities

Table 2. Evaluation of Hydride Donors<sup>a</sup>  
![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
<sup>a</sup>Reaction conditions: 1a (0.1 mmol), 2 (0.15 mmol), Pd-PEPPSI $\mathrm { I P r } ^ { \mathrm { B I D E A } }$ (2.5 mol %), $\mathrm { C s } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C O } _ { 3 }$ (0.15 mmol), THF (1.0 mL), 100 °C, 3 h under Ar. The 3a/4a yields were determined by <sup>1</sup>H NMR analysis of the crude mixtures using mesitylene as an internal standard.

were observed in these cases, demonstrating the distinct reactivity of 3,3-dimethylallyl Bpin. Although previous reports show that formic acid or formate could act as an eficient hydride donor in terminal-selective allylic reduction,<sup>20</sup> significant amounts of internal fluoroalkenes were observed in the ring-opening hydrodefluorination of gem-F CPs.

Mechanistic Studies. We next sought to elucidate the origins of the excellent and intriguing regioselectivity observed in this ring-opening hydrodefluorination reaction. A plausible reaction mechanism is proposed in Figure 1. First, Pd-catalyzed oxidative addition of gem-F CPs and subsequent defluorination afords the 2-fluorinated π-allyl Pd(II) intermediate B, a process that has been computationally established with the Pd-PEPPSI-IMes precatalyst in our previous work.<sup>9j</sup> Then, transmetalation of the allylB(pin) reagent leads to Pd-bisallyl complex C. The transmetalation step is likely assisted by the carbonate base, which activates the carbon nucleophile through coordination to the boron atom. Our potential energy surface scans show that the base-assisted transmetalation is barrierless and energetically downhill from the cationic Pd-ally species. This result can be rationalized by noting the Pd-allyl moiety renders the Pd center more electron deficient, which promotes transmetalation.

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
Figure 1. Plausible mechanism of the Pd-catalyzed regioselective hydrodefluorination of gem-F CPs.

After the transmetalation step, hydride transfer can occur in two diferent ways. One is to first undergo a β-hydride elimination to generate a Pd hydride species D, followed by reductive elimination. Another is a direct 3,4′-hydride transfer through the bis-η<sup>1</sup>-coordinated structure E. Because diferent hydride donors exhibited very diferent regioselectivities, we hypothesized that the hydride transfer was unlikely to proceed through a common Pd hydride intermediate species. Indeed, our potential energy surface scans indicated that for 3,3- dimethylallyl Bpin, the β-hydride elimination process is extremely unfavorable and energetically all the way uphill. Even if the Pd hydride species is formed, calculated hydride transfer transition states (Figure 2) indicate that the internal (thermodynamic) alkene regioisomer would be favored due to

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
Figure 2. Calculated hydride transfer transition states through a Pd hydride species with Pd-2 at the M06/def2-TZVPP-SDD(Pd), SMD(THF)//B3LYP-D3/def2-SVP−LANL2DZ(Pd) level of theory. Energies are in kcal/mol. Interatomic distances are in Å.

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
Figure 4. Mechanistic experiments.

the lower free energy barrier of TS-2-Z, not the terminal (kinetic) alkene regioisomer that is experimentally observed. This mechanistic pathway is therefore unlikely to be operational for 3,3-dimethylallyl Bpin. Transition states for the direct 3,4′-hydride transfer through a bis-η<sup>1</sup>-coordinated structure (Figure 3) indicate that this alternative mechanistic pathway would explain the experimental regioselectivity very well. The lowest-energy transition state leading to the terminal alkene regioisomer, TS-3b-E, is 1.5 kcal/mol more favorable than the lowest-energy transition state leading to the thermodynamic alkene regioisomer, TS-4b-E. This is in very good agreement with the experimental product ratio (65:4) which corresponds to a 1.6 kcal/mol diference in transition state energies. It is also worth noting that this hydride transfer process generates isoprene, which presumably escapes as a gas under the reaction conditions, rendering this transformation overall irreversible. This helps to further rationalize why the terminal (kinetic) alkene product is not equilibrated back to its internal (thermodynamic) isomer.

Table 3. Substrate Scope with Respect to the gem-F CPs<sup>ab</sup>  
![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
<sup>a</sup>Reaction conditions: 1 (0.1 mmol), 2a (0.15 mmol), Pd-PEPPSI-IPr<sup>BIDEA</sup> (2.5 mol %), Cs CO (0.15 mmol), THF (1.0 mL), 100 °C, 3 h under Ar. <sup>b</sup>Isolated yields. The terminal/internal ratios (T/I) were determined by <sup>1</sup>H NMR analysis of the isolated products.

We also performed control experiments to shed further light on the mechanistic pathway (Figure 4). Both E- and Z-2- fluorocarbonates aforded the corresponding terminal alkene 3a in 94% and 96% yields, respectively, indicating the formation of 2-fluorinated π-allyl Pd(II) intermediate B (Figure 4A). In addition, volatile isoprene was detected in 78% yield by crude <sup>1</sup>H NMR, confirming that 3,3-dimethallyl Bpin acted as the hydride donor (Figure 4B). When the analogous gem-dichlorocyclopropane or gem-bromochlorocy clopropane was tested under the standard conditions, no desired ring-opening hydrodehalogenation products were detected, implying the indispensability of two fluorine atoms in the ring-opening process (Figure 4C). Furthermore, the reaction of gem-F CP 1a with 3-methylallyl Bpin or 1- methylallyl Bpin gave predominantly the terminal fluoroalkene 3a, whereas the treatment of 1a with isopropyl boronate, tert butyl boronate, or 1-vinylcyclopropyl boronate did not give any reduced product, indicating that both the pendant alkenes and the flexible alkyl moieties are essential. The lack of regioselectivity is attributed to the dificulty of transmetalation or rapid β-H elimination in the case of prenyl boronate<sup>5c,e</sup> (Figure 4D). In addition, ring-opening formation of the π-allyl palladium and bis-π-allyl palladium species<sup>16b,21</sup> were also

Scheme 2. Application of 3,3-Dimethylallyl Bpin as the Hydride Donor in Allylic and Propargyl Reductions

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)  
<sup>a</sup>Condition A: 15 (0.1 mmol), 2a (0.15 mmol), Pd-PEPPSI-IPr (2.5 mol %), ${ \mathrm { C s } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C O } } _ { 3 }$ (0.15 mmol). THF (1.0 mL). 80 $^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , 3 { - } 1 2$ h under Ar Condition B: 15f (0.1 mmol), 2a (0.15 mmol), Pd(OAc) (5 mol %), Xantphos (10 mol %), CsF (0.15 mmol), THF (1.0 mL), 80 °C, 1 h unde Ar. The terminal/internal ratios (T/I) were determined by ¹H NMR analysis of the isolated products

detected by the HRMS, further supporting the above mechanism (Figure 4E).

Investigation of the Substrate Scope. Owing to the facile synthesis of the gem- $\mathbf { \cdot F } _ { 2 } \mathbf { C P s }$ through cyclopropanation between alkenes and $\breve { \mathrm { T M S C F } _ { 3 } } , ^ { 2 2 }$ we then investigated the substrate scope of gem-F CPs under the optimized reaction conditions (Table 3). In general, hydrodefluorination with 3,3- dimethylallyl Bpin aforded the terminal fluoroalkene products in moderate to good yields with high regioselectivity. For example, gem- $\mathbf { \cdot F } _ { 2 } \mathbf { C P s }$ bearing an $^ { o - , }$ , m-, or p-methyl group on the aryl ring gave the corresponding products 3c−3e in 61− 91% yields. Functional groups such as <sup>t</sup>Bu (3f), phenyl (3g), methoxy (3h), benzyloxy (3i), N,N-diphenyl (3j), tosyl (3k), and N-acetyl (3l) were compatible under the reaction conditions. gem-F CPs derived from indole, ferrocene, phenyl pyridine, 1- naphthalene, and carbazole were all suitable substrates, delivering the desired products 3m−3q in 57−78% yields. When alkyl-substituted gem-F CP such as 1-((2,2- difluorocyclopropyl)methyl)-4-methoxybenzene was tested, conjugated fluorodiene 3r was instead obtained in 81% yield. The Pd/NHC system was also able to activate sterically hindered 1,1-disubstituted $g e m \mathrm { - } \mathrm { F } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C P s } \left( 3 s \mathrm { - } 3 \mathbf { u } \right)$ . Unfortunately, trisubstituted $g e m { \cdot } \mathrm { F } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C P s }$ derived from 1H-indene and tetrahydro-1,1′-biphenyl failed to undergo the hydrodefluorination reaction, while the defluorinative ring expansion products 3v and 3w were instead obtained in 69% and 78% yields, respectively. This result was probably due to ring strain, which might have inhibited the formation of $\mathbf { b i s } \ – \eta ^ { 1 } .$ coordinated structure E (Figure 1). Notably, the synthetic potential of this protocol has been further demonstrated through the modification of pharmaceutical and bioactive molecules. For example, the terminal fluoroalkene moiety could be eficiently incorporated into the skeletons of menthol (3x), diacetone-glucose (3y), dihydrocholesterol (3z), and α- tocopherol (3aa).

Application in Allylic and Propargyl Reduction. Having demonstrated the ability of $^ { 3 , 3 }$ -dimethylallyl boronate to act as an eficient and regioselective hydride donor, we next explored the reduction with allylic substrates (Scheme 2). We were pleased to find that hydrodechlorination, hydrodeoxygenation, and hydrodesulfonation also occurred, delivering the corresponding terminal alkenes 16a−16e in 61%−96% yields. In addition, the chemoselective reduction of propargylic carbonate with 3,3-dimethylallyl boronate was also achieved, with allene product 16f obtained in 41% yield. Furthermore, the ring-opening reduction products 16g and 16h were obtained in 56% and 60% yield, respectively, when vinyloxirane and vinylcyclopropane were tested. This protocol could also be extended to the Pd-catalyzed decarboxylative reduction of vinyl dioxolanone (16i, 81%) and vinyl oxazolinone (16j, 50%). These results further demonstrated the synthetic value of 3,3- dimethylallyl boronate in regioselective reductions.

In summary, we showed that a well-defined Pd-IPr<sup>BIDEA</sup> complex could be used to catalyze the regioselective hydro defluorination of gem-F CPs with 3,3-dimethylallyl Bpin as a novel hydride donor. With this new strategy, a variety of terminal fluoroalkenes were obtained in good to excellent yields. The use of 3,3-dimethallyl Bpin as a competent and selective reductant in other types of allylic reductions has also been established. Experiments and DFT calculations both indicate that a direct 3,4′-hydride transfer mechanism through a bis-η<sup>1</sup>-coordinated Pd species is operative and responsible for the unusual regioselectivity. This study unlocked the potential of allyl boronates to serve as robust hydride synthons in addition to their classic roles as nucleophiles and transmetalation reagents. Additional eforts to expand the scope and utility of this reactivity are currently underway in our laboratory.

## ■ ASSOCIATED CONTENT

Asymmetric Transition Metal-Catalyzed Allylic Alkylations: Applications in Total Synthesis. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 2921−2944. see:. Diner, C.; Szabó, K. J. Recent Advances in the Preparation and Application of Allylboron Species in Organic Synthesis. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2017, 139, 2−14.
